content:
What would be the best way to handle this enum:

enum hrtimer_mode {
    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS    = 0x00,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL    = 0x01,
    HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED = 0x02,
    HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT   = 0x04,
    HRTIMER_MODE_HARD   = 0x08,

    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED = HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED = HRTIMER_MODE_REL | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED,

    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_SOFT   = HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL_SOFT   = HRTIMER_MODE_REL | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT,

    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED_SOFT = HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED_SOFT = HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT,

    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_HARD   = HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL_HARD   = HRTIMER_MODE_REL | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD,

    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED_HARD = HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED_HARD = HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD,
};

My naive approach would be something like this:

pub enum TimerMode {
    /// Timer expires at the given expiration time.
    Absolute,
    /// Timer expires after the given expiration time interpreted as a duration from now.
    Relative,
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    Pinned,
    /// Timer handler is executed in soft irq context
    Soft,
    /// Timer handler is executed in hard irq context
    Hard,
    /// Timer expires at the given expiration time.
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    AbsolutePinned,
    /// Timer expires after the given expiration time interpreted as a duration from now.
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    RelativePinned,
    /// Timer expires at the given expiration time.
    /// Timer handler is executed in soft irq context
    AbsoluteSoft,
    /// Timer expires after the given expiration time interpreted as a duration from now.
    /// Timer handler is executed in soft irq context
    RelativeSoft,
    /// Timer expires at the given expiration time.
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    /// Timer handler is executed in soft irq context
    AbsolutePinnedSoft,
    /// Timer expires after the given expiration time interpreted as a duration from now.
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    /// Timer handler is executed in soft irq context
    RelativePinnedSoft,
    /// Timer expires at the given expiration time.
    /// Timer handler is executed in hard irq context
    AbsoluteHard,
    /// Timer expires after the given expiration time interpreted as a duration from now.
    /// Timer handler is executed in hard irq context
    RelativeHard,
    /// Timer expires at the given expiration time.
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    /// Timer handler is executed in hard irq context
    AbsolutePinnedHard,
    /// Timer expires after the given expiration time interpreted as a duration from now.
    /// Timer does not move between CPU cores.
    /// Timer handler is executed in hard irq context
    RelativePinnedHard,
}

impl From<TimerMode> for bindings::hrtimer_mode {
    fn from(value: TimerMode) -> Self {
        use bindings::*;
        match value {
            TimerMode::Absolute => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS,
            TimerMode::Relative => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL,
            TimerMode::Pinned => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED,
            TimerMode::Soft => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT,
            TimerMode::Hard => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_HARD,
            TimerMode::AbsolutePinned => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED,
            TimerMode::RelativePinned => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED,
            TimerMode::AbsoluteSoft => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_SOFT,
            TimerMode::RelativeSoft => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL_SOFT,
            TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedSoft => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED_SOFT,
            TimerMode::RelativePinnedSoft => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED_SOFT,
            TimerMode::AbsoluteHard => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_HARD,
            TimerMode::RelativeHard => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL_HARD,
            TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedHard => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED_HARD,
            TimerMode::RelativePinnedHard => hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL_PINNED_HARD,
        }
    }
}

It feels like there should be a way that scales better. Is there a better way?
We have asked for this from the bindgen devs

I read the zulip thread and the bindgen issue. Those seem like cool features to have.

In this particular case, the enum on C side is a set of flags and some valid combinations of those flags. I don't think the work in the links I posted handle that specifically. I was thinking something like bitflags combined with the bindgen output maybe.
I think bitflags probably won't suffice here, because you have mutual exclusive flags? E.g. HARD and SOFT
Might be better to have a struct newtype and have helper functions instead
I though a little about that as well. Like generating a builder that only has the functions to build the combinations that are allowed.
But I would rather not write that as a macro_rules macro :laughter_tears:

I don't think the work in the links I posted handle that specifically.

In what sense/way? i.e. you would get all the possible values, which is what your C side has too.

I guess you mean something different, i.e. enums that have values that are outside those (e.g. flags that can be combined in ways that the enum variants do not describe, even if you may have some named combinations). For those, you cannot use a Rust enum anyway, so you need to stick to the consts (or one of the other bindgen modes).

I though a little about that as well. Like generating a builder that only has the functions to build the combinations that are allowed.

Yeah, having some help generating that kind of thing would be nice -- ideally we would be able to annotate the C side enough to generate everything from there.

hi, lurker here,

I think that if if mode_abs wasn't 0x00, the Rust enum could trivialy be derived from the C one no?
The rust and C implementations could have identical behaviour and be easily implemented with | combinations of the bindings values, and the rust enum can easily be converted to the original value with 'as u8'

Perhaps the simplest fix would be to propose a change to the C code ? maybe shift all first five modes once or to have mode_abs be 0x10 ?

By shifting, I mean if the C enum started with

enum hrtimer_mode {
    HRTIMER_MODE_ABS    = 0x01,
    HRTIMER_MODE_REL    = 0x02,
    HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED = 0x04,
    HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT   = 0x08,
    HRTIMER_MODE_HARD   = 0x10,
and how do you guarantee that HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD is not allowed?

In rust code, so long as the enum does not implement BitOr trait, you cannot create wrong combinations.
Or do you mean how to prevent one from attempting to go from a wrong C enum combination to a non-existent rust counterpart ?
What's the difference from what Andreas proposed originally and why do we need to avoid 0x00?

If one initialises a rust enum with the same values as the C enum, there is a collision of multiple enum values because
HRTIMER_MODE_ABS|<anything> == <anything>
And so you cannot compile because the enum has, for instance,
TimerMode::Pinned == TimerMode::AbsolutePinned

Changing the values so that MODE_ABS combos have different values than without MODE_ABS allows the identical initialisation of the rust enum + then you can easily get the C value from the enum since they are the same.

Now that I think about it, it's very weird to start this enum from 0x00.
I've always done it like BIT(0), BIT(1) etc. where BIT(X) is (1) << (X)
It is a bit strange, but probably because they want the default init state to be just absolute mode?

And so you cannot compile because the enum has

What do you mean you cannot compile?
HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED has the same value as HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED
so they cannot coexist in an enum
Can't we get rid of HRTIMER_MODE_ABS_PINNED and say the default state is ABS?
Ah, I see, defining (not initializing) the Rust enum with multiple variants with the same value.

Andreas Hindborg said:

It is a bit strange, but probably because they want the default init state to be just absolute mode?

I guess.. funny thing is, I personally would expect a timer to be relative by default

I played around with this situation, and assuming a corrected (mode_abs=0x00 issue) enum definition, we can define and impl a few traits to handle BitOr ops on the Rust side in a satisfactory manner, by having a Result<TimerMode, InvalidBitsError> output, as shown in this playground

I am using this topic as an excuse to learn macros, and for initialised unit enums with a specified uint repr attribute one can generate traits impl so that all these asserts succeed:

    // Absolute and Relative do not produce a valid outcome
    assert!((TimerMode::Absolute | TimerMode::Relative).is_err());

    // Absolute and Pinned produce a valid outcome: AbsolutePinned
    assert!(
        (TimerMode::Absolute | TimerMode::Pinned).is_ok_and(|res| res == TimerMode::AbsolutePinned)
    );

    // Relative and Soft and Pinned produce a valid outcome: RelativePinnedSoft
    assert!((TimerMode::Relative | TimerMode::Soft | TimerMode::Pinned)
        .is_ok_and(|res| res == TimerMode::RelativePinnedSoft));

    // Absolute and AbsolutePinned and AbsoluteHard produce a valid outcome: AbsolutePinnedHard
    assert!(
        (TimerMode::Absolute | TimerMode::AbsolutePinned | TimerMode::AbsoluteHard)
            .is_ok_and(|res| res == TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedHard)
    );

    // Hard and Soft do not produce a valid outcome
    assert!((TimerMode::Absolute
        | TimerMode::AbsolutePinned
        | TimerMode::AbsoluteHard
        | TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedSoft)
        .is_err());

    // Although PinnedHard is not a valid outcome, when Absolute is added it becomes valid
    assert!((TimerMode::Pinned | TimerMode::Hard).is_err());
    assert!((TimerMode::Pinned | TimerMode::Hard | TimerMode::Absolute)
        .is_ok_and(|res| res == TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedHard));

    println!("Done with BitOr asserts");

    let res: Result<TimerMode, InvalidBitsError> = 1.try_into();

    assert_eq!(res, Ok(TimerMode::Absolute));

    assert_eq!(2.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::Relative));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(3), Err(InvalidBitsError(3)));  // This would be
// HRTIMER_MODE_ABS = 0x01 + HRTIMER_MODE_REL = 0x02,
    assert_eq!(4.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::Pinned));
    assert_eq!(5.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::AbsolutePinned));
    assert_eq!(6.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::RelativePinned));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(7), Err(InvalidBitsError(7)));
    assert_eq!(8.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::Soft));
    assert_eq!(9.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::AbsoluteSoft));
    assert_eq!(10.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::RelativeSoft));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(7), Err(InvalidBitsError(7)));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(7), Err(InvalidBitsError(7)));
    assert_eq!(13.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedSoft));
    assert_eq!(14.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::RelativePinnedSoft));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(7), Err(InvalidBitsError(7)));
    assert_eq!(16.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::Hard));
    assert_eq!(17.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::AbsoluteHard));
    assert_eq!(18.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::RelativeHard));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(19), Err(InvalidBitsError(19)));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(20), Err(InvalidBitsError(20)));
    assert_eq!(21.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::AbsolutePinnedHard));
    assert_eq!(22.try_into(), Ok(TimerMode::RelativePinnedHard));
    assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(23), Err(InvalidBitsError(23)));
    for bits in 23..=u8::MAX{
        assert_eq!(TimerMode::try_from(bits), Err(InvalidBitsError(bits)));
    }

    println!("Done with TryInto/TryFrom asserts");

@Ariel Miculas this guarantee that HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD is not allowed while still implementing the BitOr trait.
Nice work, though we'll have to ask Andreas if it's acceptable to change the values of these constants so we can get rid of 0x00.

I’m out of office this week, I’ll take a look next week. Sounds like something to discuss with upstream timer subsystem though. Perhaps something to fix after initial hrtimer abstractions are upstream?

:+1: I wonder how many users used ints directly, or relied on the assumption of unspecified == absolute

in your branch hrtimer-v3-v6.12-rc2:

grep -r HRTIMER_MODE --exclude-dir .git | wc -l
527
I guess it also matters how many people are actually |'ing it. From a few quick greps, I only found a few cases doing |=.
It would also be nice to be able to do this in const even if the optimizers will see through it, but impl const is unstable.

Another note: depending on the use cases for something like this, perhaps generating a typestate/builder approach would be better (or as an alternative for those that don't need the runtime part), as long as all it is all generated etc.
hmm I am wondering how good it is ergonomically for | to return a Result.
probably a newtype struct with builder & or getter/setter functions makes more sense

BitOr is also implemented with Result<TimerMode, Err> for the right hand side so I think its nice. An important aspect is to ensure that someone does not produce an invalid combination.

It allows nearly the same | ergonomics as the C side, with validity checking

I wonder though if something like this is just as good, if not better, since no runtime errors are even possible:
TimerMode::absolute().pinned(true).hard(true)

It does seems good if not better. A bit harder to derive from the enum though. What happens when:

TimerMode::absolute().pinned(true).hard(true)
is supposed to be equal to (at least in the C api) :
TimerMode::pinned(true).hard(true)

Do we allow ?
TimerMode::pinned(true).hard(true).relative(true)
depends on how complicated we want the typestate to be
I don't know what timer mode actually is, so I don't know what you are referring to, but you can make it always overwrite the value, or not support the function on the respective state

high-resolution kernel timershttps://docs.kernel.org/timers/hrtimers.html#hrtimers-subsystem-for-high-resolution-kernel-timers

A builder requires to decode a semantic from the C enum to make a nice (and arguably better) builder system (the encoded semantic is why it is nicer).
The BitOr version just checks if the resulting bits is in the valid set to choose whether to return Ok(transmute...) or Err(..) so it is dumber but also agnostic.

I'll try my hand at a builder too, to see if I can make it work.

If we are going to do something fancy like one of these approaches, then we don't need to change the C values, i.e. we could track validity ourselves and then produce the right value at the end (e.g. via typestate if we use that, or if we don't, via storing e.g. a "plus one" value internally).

I also realised that my approach ends up with Pinned, Hard, or Soft as a valid states on their own when they might only be valid in C because they are equivalent to AbsolutePinned, AbsoluteHard, AbsoluteSoft.
I think my approach only works if we get rid of Absolute in Rust and consider all non-Relative to be Absolute-by-default. In which case we do not have to change the C values either, in fact we need the 0x00 value to indicate the Default.

I see the wisdom in Benno's and Miguel's words :)

Following a typestate builder schema, here where a builder could currently look like.
I assumed soft and hard are mutually exclusive, but i am not sure about that. If it was turned into macro the user would be required input the mutual-exclusion information.

# At this stage, only with_<absolute|relative|pinned|unpinned|soft|hard> methods are available

> let mut mode_builder = mode_builder.with_absolute();
> mode_builder
Builder { flags: [Some(0), None, None], t: PhantomData<...> }

# now set_absolute a set_relative are available
> mode_builder.set_relative();
> mode_builder
Builder { flags: [Some(1), None, None], t: PhantomData<...> }


> let mut mode_builder = mode_builder.with_pinned();
> mode_builder
Builder { flags: [Some(1), Some(2), None], t: PhantomData<...> }

# now set_pinned a set_unpinned are available
> mode_builder.set_unpinned();
> mode_builder
Builder { flags: [Some(1), Some(0), None], t: PhantomData<...> }


# for now the `.build()` method is not available because we still haven't set hard or soft

> let mut mode_builder = mode_builder.with_hard();
> mode_builder
Builder { flags: [Some(1), Some(0), Some(8)], t: PhantomData<...> }


# now set_hard a set_soft are available
> mode_builder.set_soft();
> mode_builder
Builder { flags: [Some(1), Some(0), Some(4)], t: PhantomData<...> }


# now `.build()` is available and results in:
> mode_builder.build()
TimerMode(5)
> HRTIMER_MODE_REL_SOFT
5


# we can queue the function calls of course:
> let mode_builder = builder::TimerMode::builder()
    .with_soft()
    .with_relative()
    .with_unpinned();

> mode_builder.build()
TimerMode(5)
> HRTIMER_MODE_REL_SOFT
5

the Rust code that does this:

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct TimerMode(u8);
impl TimerMode {
    pub fn builder() -> Builder<Missing<AbsoluteRelative>, Missing<Pinned>, Missing<HardSoft>> {
        Builder {
            flags: Default::default(),
            t: std::marker::PhantomData,
        }
    }
}

#[derive(Debug)]
#[repr(C)]
/// If I understand correcly, when it is Repr(C) is is safe to transmute the builder to change only
/// unit generics held as PhandomData. If it is not Repr(C) there are no solid garanties that the
/// memory layout does not change depending on the unit generic.
/// A safe alternative is to make new different instance with the right generics and copy/move the
/// flags into it. If creating TimerModes is not a hot path then maybe the safer way is best.
pub struct Builder<S1, S2, S3> {
    flags: [Option<u8>; 3],
    t: std::marker::PhantomData<(S1, S2, S3)>,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Missing<T> {
    t: std::marker::PhantomData<T>,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Valid<T> {
    t: std::marker::PhantomData<T>,
}

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct AbsoluteRelative;
#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Pinned;
#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct HardSoft;

impl<S2, S3> Builder<Valid<AbsoluteRelative>, S2, S3> {
    pub fn set_absolute(&mut self) {
        self.flags[0] = Some(HRTIMER_MODE_ABS);
    }
    pub fn set_relative(&mut self) {
        self.flags[0] = Some(HRTIMER_MODE_REL);
    }
}

impl<S1, S3> Builder<S1, Valid<Pinned>, S3> {
    pub fn set_pinned(&mut self) {
        self.flags[1] = Some(HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED);
    }
    pub fn set_unpinned(&mut self) {
        self.flags[1] = Some(0);
    }
}

impl<S1, S2> Builder<S1, S2, Valid<HardSoft>> {
    pub fn set_hard(&mut self) {
        self.flags[2] = Some(HRTIMER_MODE_HARD);
    }
    pub fn set_soft(&mut self) {
        self.flags[2] = Some(HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT);
    }
}

impl Builder<Valid<AbsoluteRelative>, Valid<Pinned>, Valid<HardSoft>> {
    pub fn build(self) -> TimerMode {
        TimerMode(self.flags.iter().flatten().sum::<u8>())
    }
}

impl<S2, S3> Builder<Missing<AbsoluteRelative>, S2, S3> {
    pub fn with_absolute(self) -> Builder<Valid<AbsoluteRelative>, S2, S3> {
        let mut b: Builder<Valid<AbsoluteRelative>, S2, S3> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(self) };
        b.set_absolute();
        b
    }

    pub fn with_relative(self) -> Builder<Valid<AbsoluteRelative>, S2, S3> {
        let mut b: Builder<Valid<AbsoluteRelative>, S2, S3> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(self) };
        b.set_relative();
        b
    }
}

impl<S1, S3> Builder<S1, Missing<Pinned>, S3> {
    pub fn with_pinned(self) -> Builder<S1, Valid<Pinned>, S3> {
        let mut b: Builder<S1, Valid<Pinned>, S3> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(self) };
        b.set_pinned();
        b
    }

    pub fn with_unpinned(self) -> Builder<S1, Valid<Pinned>, S3> {
        let mut b: Builder<S1, Valid<Pinned>, S3> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(self) };
        b.set_unpinned();
        b
    }
}
impl<S1, S2> Builder<S1, S2, Missing<HardSoft>> {
    pub fn with_hard(self) -> Builder<S1, S2, Valid<HardSoft>> {
        let mut b: Builder<S1, S2, Valid<HardSoft>> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(self) };
        b.set_hard();
        b
    }

    pub fn with_soft(self) -> Builder<S1, S2, Valid<HardSoft>> {
        let mut b: Builder<S1, S2, Valid<HardSoft>> = unsafe { std::mem::transmute(self) };
        b.set_soft();
        b
    }
}

fn main() {
    let mut mode_builder = TimerMode::builder().with_absolute().with_hard();
    mode_builder.set_soft();
    let mode = mode_builder.with_unpinned().build();
    println!("{:?}", mode);
}

Timothy G. said:

/// If I understand correcly, when it is Repr(C) is is safe to transmute the builder to change only
/// unit generics held as PhandomData. If it is not Repr(C) there are no solid garanties that the
/// memory layout does not change depending on the unit generic.
/// A safe alternative is to make new different instance with the right generics and copy/move the
/// flags into it. If creating TimerModes is not a hot path then maybe the safer way is best.

I am pretty sure that copying the flags will get optimized away, but we should definitely check before we use that
but otherwise you are correct about the layout
I am wondering if we should have default values, eg Absolute is the default. Also we should consider removing the set_ prefix, as I don't think that it adds value.

I have tried making a proc macro that generates such a namedflag(bits) wrapper with its builder + a Try_From impl,
can be found here https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/compare/rust-next...GTimothy:linux:bitflag

with it, this runs:

pub(crate) mod bindings {
    pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_ABS: u8 = 0x00;
    pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_REL: u8 = 0x01;
    pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED: u8 = 0x02;
    pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT: u8 = 0x04;
    pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_HARD: u8 = 0x08;
}

use bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_HARD;

bitflag! [
    name: TimerMode,
    type: u8,
    groups_of_incompatible: {
        AbsRel:{
        absolute: bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_ABS,
        relative: bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_REL,
        },
        Pin:{
        pinned: bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED,
        unpinned: 0, // setting 0 bits: unpinned will behave like a default in try_from, like Absolute is the default for AbsRel.
        },
        SoftHard:{
        soft: bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT, //with path
        hard: HRTIMER_MODE_HARD, //without path
    },
    },
];

use bindings::{HRTIMER_MODE_ABS, HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED, HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT};
let flag_builder = TimerMode::builder();
let flag = flag_builder
    .with_absolute()
    .with_pinned()
    .with_soft()
    .build();
assert_eq!(
    flag.bits(), // <TimerMode as BitFlag>::bits(&flag),
    bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT
);

let res: Result<TimerMode, u8> =
    (HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD).try_into();
assert_eq!(
    res,
    Err(HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD),
    "HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD are incompatible, this should fail"
);

let res: Result<TimerMode, u8> = (HRTIMER_MODE_ABS).try_into();
assert_eq!(
    res,
    Err(HRTIMER_MODE_ABS),
    "we need to specify whether pinned or not, and whether soft or hard, this should fail"
);

Benno Lossin said:

I am wondering if we should have default values, eg Absolute is the default. Also we should consider removing the set_ prefix, as I don't think that it adds value.

in this try_from implementation, flags parts with 0 bits always match and behave like defaults.
The set_ prefix were to contrast the with_ prefix. I meant them to mean "I am now changing something that was already set". It can be changed :)
Very cool!
I think .absolute(true) is a bit closer to the patterns used in std than the .with_absolute() one. For instance for the OpenOptions.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think .absolute(true) is a bit closer to the patterns used in std than the .with_absolute() one. For instance for the OpenOptions.

I see. I think the case of OpenOptions is closer to what I would call 'true bitflags' where every option is additive, but here in the C code, one should not add Soft and Hard if i understand correctly. If so, i think to stick to the OpenOptions logic, I would get rid of either relative or absolute, and unpinned, and either soft or hard.

What happens for groups with 3+ choices (it is not the case for hrtimer_mode but maybe for other flags) when once calls choice1(false)? Does that happen at all ?

right now the proc macro works for arbitrary number of options in a given group, but my code just has each option overwrite the same Option<u8> value. For a binary choice with True/False parameters, the logic needs to change a little so that the main option needs to be aware of the other option value to set when it is called with main_option(false)

Timothy G. said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I think .absolute(true) is a bit closer to the patterns used in std than the .with_absolute() one. For instance for the OpenOptions.

I see. I think the case of OpenOptions is closer to what I would call 'true bitflags' where every option is additive, but here in the C code, one should not add Soft and Hard if i understand correctly. If so, i think to stick to the OpenOptions logic, I would get rid of either relative or absolute, and unpinned, and either soft or hard.

What happens for groups with 3+ choices (it is not the case for hrtimer_mode but maybe for other flags) when once calls choice1(false)? Does that happen at all ?

right now the proc macro works for arbitrary number of options in a given group, but my code just has each option overwrite the same Option<u8> value. For a binary choice with True/False parameters, the logic needs to change a little so that the main option needs to be aware of the other option value to set when it is called with main_option(false)

That makes sense :+1: I did not think of that.

I have made a variant for binary choices (which is the case for TimerMode).
It behaves much more like fs::OpenOptions, with .<option>(bool) methods.
Right now it is a proc_macro but I think it could be a rules_macro.

    pub(crate) mod bindings {
        pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_ABS: u8 = 0x00;
        pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_REL: u8 = 0x01;
        pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED: u8 = 0x02;
        pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT: u8 = 0x04;
        pub(crate) const HRTIMER_MODE_HARD: u8 = 0x08;
    }

    use bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_HARD;

    bitflag_options! [
    name: TimerMode,
    type: u8,
    options: {
            relative: bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_REL : bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_ABS,
            pinned: bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED : 0,
            hard: HRTIMER_MODE_HARD : bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT,
    },
    ];

    use bindings::{HRTIMER_MODE_ABS, HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED, HRTIMER_MODE_REL, HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT};
    let tmode: TimerMode = TimerMode::try_from(bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT)
        .expect("other settings have 0bit modes so they should all match");

    assert_eq!(tmode, TimerMode::default().hard(false));
    assert_eq!(tmode, TimerMode::default());

    assert_eq!(
        TimerMode::default().hard(true).pinned(true).relative(true),
        TimerMode::try_from(HRTIMER_MODE_HARD | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_REL)
            .expect("this is a valid combination of flags")
    );

    assert_eq!(
        TimerMode::default()
            .hard(false)
            .pinned(true)
            .relative(false),
        TimerMode::try_from(HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_ABS)
            .expect("this is a valid combination of flags")
    );

    let flag = TimerMode::default().pinned(true).hard(false);
    assert_eq!(
        flag.bits(), // <TimerMode as BitFlag>::bits(&flag),
        bindings::HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT
    );

    let res: Result<TimerMode, u8> =
        (HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD).try_into();
    assert_eq!(
        res,
        Err(HRTIMER_MODE_ABS | HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED | HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD),
        "HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT | HRTIMER_MODE_HARD are incompatible, this should fail"
    );

    let res: Result<TimerMode, u8> = (HRTIMER_MODE_ABS).try_into();
    assert_eq!(
        res,
        Err(HRTIMER_MODE_ABS),
        "Although unpinned matches because it has a flag of 0, we still need to specify whether whether soft or hard"
    );

Timothy G. said:

Right now it is a proc_macro but I think it could be a rules_macro.

Made a macro_rules that passes the same asserts:

   bitflag_options_rules! {
    name: TimerMode,
    type: u32,
    options: [
        {name: relative, true: bindings::hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_REL, false: bindings::hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_ABS},
        {name: pinned, true: bindings::hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_PINNED, false: 0_u32},
        {name: hard, true: hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_HARD, false: bindings::hrtimer_mode_HRTIMER_MODE_SOFT}
    ]
}
Very cool! I would expect the proc_macro version to be easier to reason about? Did you use out of tree dependencies for the proc macro?
Or did you rely on syn for the proc macro?

No, I just imitated the modules proc_macro at first for the initial typestate builder schema bitflag proc_macro.

Then the OpenOptions-like versionbitflag_options proc_macro is simpler since I got rid of typestate building (because it has default values it is always in a valid state + calling relative(true).relative(false) is equal to calling relative(false)).

Finally, since I got rid of the typestate builder, i did not need to use proc_macro for the compicated variations of <(Valid|Invalid)+> states of the builder. It ends up fitting in a much simpler and cleaner in my opinion:

macro_rules! bitflag_options_rules {{

    name:$name:ident,
    type:$t:ty,
    options:[
        $({name:$fn_name:ident, true: $true_bitval:expr, false: $false_bitval:expr}),+
]} => {

        #[derive(Debug, PartialEq)]
        pub struct $name($t);

        impl BitFlag for $name {
        type Bits = $t;

        fn bits(&self) -> Self::Bits {
            self.0
        }
    }

    impl $name {
    $(pub fn $fn_name(mut self, $fn_name:bool)->Self{
            if $fn_name{
                self.0 = self.0 & !$false_bitval | $true_bitval;
            }else{
                self.0 = self.0 & !$true_bitval | $false_bitval;
            }
            self
        })+


    }

    impl Default for $name{
        fn default() -> Self {
            Self(0)$(.$fn_name(false))+
        }
    }

    impl TryFrom<<$name as BitFlag>::Bits> for $name {
        type Error = <$name as BitFlag>::Bits;

        fn try_from(value: <$name as BitFlag>::Bits) -> Result<Self, Self::Error> {
            let mut to_process = value.clone();

            let mut $(
            matched = false;

            for flag in [$true_bitval, $false_bitval] {
                if (flag & to_process) == flag {
                    matched = true;
                    to_process -= flag;
                    if flag > 0 {
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            if !matched {
                return Err(value);
            })+


            if to_process == 0 {
                return Ok(Self(value));
            }
            return Err(value)
        }
    }

    };
}

Andreas Hindborg said:

Very cool! I would expect the proc_macro version to be easier to reason about?

The proc_macro could be easier to reason about for the code generation part, but is a big string build + token stream conversion. But, the TokenStream hand parsing is probably more error prone and quite messy.
I think the macro_rules is easier to debug.
Also, I did not use very complicated logic in the macro_rules, just a couple of $()+ repeats.

In anycase, just expanding the macro helps understanding both versions quite well.

should i try a "good first issue" first ?

It is not required -- those are there to help people find issues that (we think) are good to start with something. Sometimes they are trivial, and are meant to help people test their email setup etc.

should I wait for HRTimer to land to have a user for the macro ?

That is the easiest way to comply with the rule of having a user, yeah. But sometimes we can upstream things if we know for sure we will have a user afterwards. So you can take the user (even if it is not yet in mainline) and show how it would look like (e.g. in your repo, or in an RFC patch that requires the user's patch series to be applied).
